item.title: Adaptive Variety
item.description: Adaptive variety is the range of distinct responses a system can deploy under stress. A forest with thirty tree species, diverse understory, and multiple mycorrhizal networks has high adaptive variety — if one species fails under drought, others persist, and the system reorganizes around what survives. A monoculture plantation has low adaptive variety — if the single crop fails, the entire system fails with it. The concept applies identically to social systems, organizational structures, and political frameworks.

item.title: Collapse Environment
item.description:
A collapse environment is defined by the simultaneous presence of five conditions that together destroy the operational prerequisites of universalist coordination. The conditions are not individually unusual — any one of them appears in ordinary crises. Their simultaneous interaction is what produces collapse: each condition amplifies the others, and frameworks designed to handle any single condition fail when all five hold at once.

Time compression. The interval between threat recognition and required response shrinks below the threshold that deliberative processes require. Storms arrive before assemblies conclude. Infrastructure fails faster than bureaucracies authorize repairs. The assumption that there will be time to decide is the first thing collapse removes.

item.title: Resilience
item.description:
Resilience is the capacity of a system to absorb disturbance and reorganize while retaining its essential function, structure, and identity. C. S. Holling introduced the concept in 1973 to distinguish it from stability — a distinction that matters politically as much as ecologically.

Stability is the tendency to remain near a single equilibrium. A stable system resists perturbation and returns to its original state. A monoculture crop field is stable in this sense: it maintains high yield under normal conditions and resists small disruptions through pesticide application and irrigation. But it collapses under large disturbance — drought, novel pest, soil depletion — because it lacks the internal diversity to reorganize. Stability and fragility go together.

item.title: Food Sovereignty
item.description: Food sovereignty is the right of peoples to define their own food systems: what they grow, how they grow it, and how food circulates within and between communities. The concept was articulated as a political framework by La Via Campesina at the World Food Summit in 1996. It goes beyond food security — which asks only whether a population has enough food — to insist on community control over agricultural practices, seed systems, land use, and the social relations of food production. A population can be food-secure while having no sovereignty over its food system, as when communities depend entirely on imported commodities or industrial supply chains they do not control.
